title: Parent / Guardian Questionnaire
intro: You know your child in ways school staff cannot. Your answers help the school understand your child's strengths, history, and needs. Approximate answers are fine - precision matters less than the overall picture. You may skip any question, and you can close this form and return later using the same secure link.

questions.prompt: What is your relationship to the child?
Who lives in your child's home with them?
questions.helpText: Relationships and ages are enough - names are not needed (for example: mother, stepfather, two younger sisters ages 4 and 6).
questions.notes: Deliberately minimal vs. source forms' full household rosters with names, education levels, and custody detail - data-minimization posture. Custody/guardianship specifics belong in the psychologist's district records, not this tool.

questions.prompt: What language(s) does your child hear and speak at home? Which did they learn first?
What are your child's strengths and interests?
questions.helpText: What do they enjoy? What are they good at? What do you love about them?

questions.prompt: What concerns you most about your child right now?
Which areas concern you?

options.label: Attention, organization, or getting things done
I'm not sure - the school raised the concern

questions.prompt: When did you first become concerned?

options.label: In the past year or two
I have not been concerned myself

questions.prompt: What questions would you like this process to answer for you?

modules.title: Birth & Early Development
modules.intro: Approximate answers are fine here. If you don't recall something, choose "don't recall" and move on.

questions.prompt: Were there any complications during the pregnancy or birth?

options.label: Not sure / I wasn't present for this period

questions.notes: Deliberately one broad question instead of the source forms' detailed prenatal checklists (substance use, maternal stress, planned/unwanted pregnancy). Those items are intrusive in a school intake and better handled in a psychologist-led interview when clinically indicated. Flag for JD review.

questions.prompt: Please describe briefly (for example: born early, breathing problems, long labor, jaundice).
Was your child born early, on time, or late?
After birth, did your child need extra medical care (such as NICU or a longer hospital stay)?
As far as you know, was your child exposed to alcohol, tobacco, or other substances before birth?
questions.helpText: This is asked of every family because prenatal exposures can affect learning and attention, and it helps the team consider all possible explanations. Answer only as far as you know - 'no information available' is a completely acceptable answer.

options.label: No, not that I know of

questions.notes: Reframed from the direct maternal-use question by JD's decision: third-person (about the child), knowledge-hedged, respondent-neutral - works for foster/adoptive/kinship caregivers, who are disproportionately the respondents in affirmative cases. 'No information available' is deliberately distinct from 'no'. Extraction fence: reported neutrally as exposure context; never characterizes any caregiver; never speculates toward FASD.

questions.prompt: Share whatever you know or were told - even general information helps.
questions.notes: Deliberately gentle open prompt - not 'what and how much'. No completeness rule nags this question (same posture as trauma item).

questions.prompt: Walking alone - compared with other children, your child walked:

options.label: Around the same time as most

questions.prompt: First words - your child spoke their first words:

options.label: Around the same time as most

questions.prompt: Short sentences - your child began combining words into short sentences:

options.label: Around the same time as most

questions.prompt: Toilet training (daytime) - your child was toilet trained:

options.label: Around the same time as most

questions.prompt: If any milestones stood out as unusually early or late, share approximate ages or details here.
Thinking about your child's first three years, check any that describe them:
questions.helpText: Many children show one or two of these. Check what fits your memory of that period.

options.label: Rarely made eye contact with people
Often didn't respond when their name was called
Didn't point at things to show interest
Didn't enjoy cuddling or was hard to comfort
Lost words or skills they previously had
Unusually intense interest in specific objects (spinning wheels, lining up toys)
Repeated the same movements often (rocking, hand-flapping)
Strong reactions to sounds, textures, or lights
Significant feeding difficulties or extreme pickiness

questions.notes: Early social-communication markers drawn from source forms' infancy sections (eye contact, name response, cuddling, object fascination) plus joint attention and regression. Extraction treats endorsements as reported observations; construct tags remain hypothesis-grade for interpretive purposes downstream - never framed as screening results.

questions.prompt: If you checked any of the above, tell us more about what you remember.
How would you describe your child's temperament as a baby and toddler?
questions.helpText: For example: easygoing, fussy, very active, cautious with new people, predictable or unpredictable routines.

questions.prompt: How would you describe your child's current overall health?
Has your child had any serious illnesses, injuries, or hospitalizations?
questions.helpText: Please include concussions (even ones described as mild), other head injuries, seizures or convulsions, very high fevers, frequent ear infections, or surgeries.
questions.notes: Concussion named explicitly as a recall cue - parents often don't file 'concussion' under 'head injury' (adopted from ChatGPT review; cue over separate question).

questions.prompt: Please describe what happened and roughly how old your child was.
Does your child have any current medical or mental-health diagnoses?
Please list the diagnoses and roughly when they were made.
Does your child currently take any medications?
What is the medication for, and have you noticed effects (helpful or not)?
questions.helpText: The purpose and effect matter most; you may include or omit medication names as you prefer.

questions.prompt: Vision: has your child's vision been checked, and are there concerns?

options.label: Checked - wears glasses or has a known concern

questions.prompt: Hearing: has your child's hearing been checked, and are there concerns?
questions.helpText: Include history of frequent ear infections or ear tubes if relevant.

options.label: Checked - known concern (or history of ear infections/tubes)

questions.prompt: Sleep: check any that apply to your child.

options.label: Seems tired even after a full night's sleep
Very hard to wake in the morning

questions.prompt: Has your child ever received counseling or therapy services (counseling, speech, occupational, or physical therapy)?
What kind, roughly when, and did it help?
Has your child ever lost abilities they had previously developed (such as language, toileting, motor skills, social engagement, or academic skills), even if those abilities later returned?
questions.notes: Lifetime regression question. Placed in Health rather than Development deliberately: Development asks how development unfolded; Health asks whether development ever changed course. Wording refined per ChatGPT review (fuller example list, 'even if abilities returned'). Catches post-illness, post-injury, seizure-related, and emotional regression at any age; extraction flags any yes for psychologist attention.

questions.prompt: What did they lose, roughly when, and did the abilities come back?

modules.title: Social Communication & Routines
modules.intro: These questions help us understand your child's social style and preferences. Many children show a few of these - check what fits.

questions.prompt: Check any that describe your child now:

options.label: Strong need for routines; gets very upset when plans change
Extremely intense interest in specific topics or objects
Repeats certain phrases, sounds, or movements
Takes things very literally; jokes and sarcasm are hard
Difficulty reading facial expressions or body language
Strongly prefers playing or being alone
Unusual sensitivity to noise, lights, textures, or tastes
Seeks out intense movement, pressure, or sensory input

questions.prompt: If you checked any, describe what you notice.
When your child was younger, how much did they engage in pretend play (feeding a doll, playing house, imaginary games)?
When your child plays or spends free time, which of these fit? (Think about now, or when they were younger if they've outgrown play.)

options.label: Plays near other kids but not really with them

questions.notes: Play-style window (adopted from ChatGPT review, trimmed from its 3-5 open questions to one tagged multi-select + one select). Solitary/parallel/cooperative maps to social development; play type maps to cognition and interests.

questions.prompt: In play with other kids, does your child tend to direct the play or follow along?

options.label: Usually directs - play has to go their way
Mostly plays alone, so it rarely comes up

questions.notes: Directing-vs-following speaks to flexibility and social reciprocity.

questions.prompt: How would you describe your child's friendships?

options.label: Has friends, including at least one close friend
Has some friends but few close ones
Wants friends but struggles to make or keep them

questions.prompt: In social situations, does your child struggle with any of these?

options.label: Starting a conversation or joining play
Working out disagreements with other kids

questions.prompt: How does your child get along with family members at home (siblings, parents, others)?
Did your child attend preschool, pre-K, or daycare? How did it go?
How many schools has your child attended (including the current one)?
Has your child ever changed schools because of academic, behavioral, disciplinary, or safety reasons?
questions.notes: Distinct from the mobility count: educational disruption vs. mere mobility. 'Safety' and 'disciplinary' adopted from ChatGPT review (bullying, expulsion). 'Family reasons' deliberately declined: it re-collapses the distinction (military transfers are family reasons and are the non-signal), and foster/DV moves are already caught by PAR-FAM-001 with its prefer-not protections.

questions.prompt: Briefly, what happened?
Has your child ever repeated or skipped a grade?
Which grade, and what led to that decision?
Has your child ever received any of these services?

options.label: Early Intervention / Child Find (before school age)
Reading or math intervention at school

questions.prompt: Has your child ever been evaluated outside of school (by a doctor, psychologist, or clinic)?
Who evaluated them, roughly when, and what did you learn? (You can also share the report with the school psychologist directly.)
How does homework usually go at your house?
How does your child feel about school?

modules.title: Home & Family Context
modules.intro: These questions help the school understand your child's world outside school. They are asked of every family. You may skip anything, and "prefer not to answer" is always respected.

questions.prompt: Have there been major changes or stressful events in your child's life in the past few years (moves, family separation, a loss, new siblings, serious illness in the family)?
If you're comfortable, briefly describe what happened and how your child responded.
questions.notes: Deliberately no completeness rule nags this question. Broad question first, no detailed trauma probes - deeper exploration belongs in a psychologist-led conversation (per planning decision on sensitive-topic gating).

questions.prompt: Is there any family history (parents, siblings, grandparents, aunts/uncles) of the following?
questions.helpText: This is asked because some learning and attention patterns run in families. It helps the team understand context - it is never used to judge anyone.

options.label: Anxiety, depression, or other emotional difficulties

questions.notes: Family history is contextual information about relatives, not evidence about the child. Extraction stores it as context-only; it must never generate construct-tagged Evidence about the student.

questions.prompt: Is there anything in your child's earlier life experiences that you believe still affects them today?
What do you notice when your child reads at home, or when you read together?
Does your child understand a story better when someone reads it to them than when they read it themselves?

options.label: Yes - clearly understands better when read to

questions.notes: Cross-informant pair with TCH-RDG-004 (listening vs. reading comprehension) - convergence here is high-value evidence.

questions.prompt: Does your child avoid reading or put up a fight about schoolwork at home?
What do you notice about your child's writing (letters, spelling, getting ideas on paper)?
What do you notice about your child's math (counting, math facts, homework problems)?
What happens when your child needs to start homework or a chore on their own?
How does your child handle directions with several steps ("brush your teeth, get your backpack, and meet me at the car")?
How often does your child lose track of belongings (jackets, homework, devices)?
Describe a recent time your child got frustrated or overwhelmed at home: what led up to it, and what helped?
How independent is your child with daily routines (getting ready in the morning, bedtime)?

options.label: Needs an adult alongside nearly the whole time

modules.title: Behavior at Home
modules.intro: Concrete descriptions of what you see and hear help more than general labels.

questions.prompt: Which of these do you see at home?

options.label: Refusing or ignoring what adults ask
Avoiding or escaping tasks (homework, chores, getting ready)
Withdrawing - spending long periods alone, shutting down
Very loud, wild, or hard-to-settle behavior

questions.prompt: Has any behavior put your child or someone else at risk of getting hurt?
questions.notes: Same safety gate as teacher form - yes flags the case for expedited psychologist review.

questions.prompt: Briefly describe what happened and when.
What discipline or strategies seem to work best with your child? What clearly doesn't work?
Does your child's behavior at home seem different from what the school describes? How?
questions.notes: Deliberate cross-setting question - direct input for the cross-informant synthesis stage.

questions.prompt: What does this look like at home? Describe what someone would see and hear.
How often does it happen?
What usually happens right before it starts, and how do the adults at home respond?
questions.notes: Antecedent + consequence combined into one question for the parent register (teacher form separates them).

modules.title: Social Development Deep Dive
modules.intro: Because of what you shared, we'd like to understand your child's social development over time in more detail. There are no right answers - your observations across the years are what help.

questions.prompt: As a toddler, how did your child share attention with you - pointing to show you things, bringing objects to show, or following your point?

options.label: Often - regularly shared attention this way

questions.notes: Joint attention - the single most ASD-informative early marker missing from the general checklist's pointing item nuance.

questions.prompt: How does your child respond to familiar people compared with unfamiliar people?
How does your child do in groups of children - birthday parties, teams, the lunch table?
What has your child been intensely interested in over the years, and how intense do those interests get?
How has your child's social interaction changed as they've gotten older?
questions.notes: Developmental-trajectory item - especially valuable for reevaluations; general forms rarely ask it.

questions.prompt: How does your child spend free time when they can choose anything?
How does your child handle understanding other people's feelings or points of view?
From what you see, how do these difficulties affect your child's daily life and learning?
What do you think people most misunderstand about your child?
questions.notes: Adopted from ChatGPT proposal - surfaces masking, cultural factors, giftedness, and overlooked strengths.

questions.prompt: If this process were successful, what would be different for your child one year from now?
questions.notes: Adopted from ChatGPT proposal - captures parent priorities, expectations, and intervention goals; seeds the recommendations layer. Pre-publication rewording of the earlier 'hopes' item.

questions.prompt: Is there anything else you'd like the school psychologist to know about your child?

followUps.prompt: What does that look like at home - what would someone see or hear?
followUps.triggerHint: Parent used a vague descriptor (meltdown, struggles, shuts down) without observable detail.

followUps.prompt: About how often does that happen in a typical week?
followUps.triggerHint: A concern was described with no frequency information.

followUps.prompt: When did you first notice this?
followUps.triggerHint: A concern was described with no onset information.

followUps.prompt: Is there anything that helps, even a little?
followUps.triggerHint: A concern was described with no mention of what mitigates it.

followUps.prompt: Of everything you mentioned, what worries you most?
followUps.triggerHint: Concern areas were selected but the primary concern was not described.

followUps.prompt: How does this compare with your other children, or with other kids their age you know?
followUps.triggerHint: A developmental or behavioral concern lacks a peer/sibling comparison anchor.

followUps.prompt: Roughly how old was your child when this happened? An approximate age is fine.
followUps.triggerHint: A developmental event was described without any age anchor.

followUps.prompt: Can you share a bit more - roughly when it happened and whether your child needed treatment?
followUps.triggerHint: A health event was endorsed without description.

followUps.prompt: Think of the most recent time: what was happening right before, and what happened right after?
followUps.triggerHint: A behavior was described without antecedent or consequence information.

followUps.prompt: How do these difficulties affect your child's everyday life - school, friendships, family time?
followUps.triggerHint: Concerns were described but daily-life impact was not addressed.

followUps.prompt: When someone reads a story to your child, do they understand it better than when they read it themselves?
followUps.triggerHint: Reading concern present but the listening-vs-reading comparison is missing.

completenessRules.description: Concern areas selected but daily-life impact not addressed.
Concern areas selected but primary concern not described.
Health event endorsed but not described.
Behavior selected but antecedent/consequence description missing.
Reading concern selected but listening-vs-reading comparison missing.

prohibited: Never assign or imply a diagnostic label (autism, ADHD, dyslexia, anxiety disorder, etc.), and never characterize social-communication endorsements as screening results or autism likelihood.
Never state or imply special education eligibility, disability category, or the need for an IEP or 504 plan.
Never make causal claims about why the child behaves or develops as reported.
Never characterize parenting quality, discipline choices, or the home environment.
Never treat 'prefer not to answer' or a skipped question as evidence of anything; record it only as information not provided.
Never generate construct-tagged evidence about the student from family-history items; family history is context about relatives only.
Never infer trauma, abuse, or adverse experiences beyond what the parent explicitly stated.
Never convert an inference into a reported fact; every statement carries its claim-type label.
Never quantify what the parent did not quantify (no invented frequencies, ages, or durations).
Never state that a specific assessment instrument will or should be administered.

Never characterize the biological mother or any caregiver in relation to prenatal exposures; report exposure information neutrally as child-history context, and never speculate toward fetal alcohol or exposure-related diagnoses.
requiredFraming: Attribute every reported statement to the parent ("The parent reported...", "The parent described...").
Preserve the parent's hedges and uncertainty (I think, around age 3, don't recall exactly) rather than strengthening them.
Frame early-development and social-communication endorsements as parent-reported observations that may warrant further assessment, not as findings.
State missing or 'prefer not to answer' information explicitly in a dedicated section, neutrally, without speculation.
Keep strengths and the parent's hopes in the summary; the parent's own questions (PAR-CORE-008) should appear so the evaluation can answer them.
Where a parent item has a teacher-form counterpart (e.g., listening-vs-reading comprehension), note agreement or difference across settings factually.
